coredata.dc:title: Curcumin Nanocapsules based on carbon dots for photodynamic sterilization towards Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Xiaoqin
Xiao, Shan
Wang, Bo
Cai, Yanxue
Chen, Xuan
Wang, Jihui
coredata.dc:description: Curcumin (Cur) as a natural food additive and photosensitizer has been widely applied on photodynamic sterilization and preservation for food, but the poor aqueous solubility and light stability restrict its extensive application. In this study, we report a Cur nanocapsules (Cur-CDs) made by carbon dots (CDs). Attributing to the hydrogen bonds formed between Cur and CDs, Cur-CDs exhibits excellent Cur aqueous solubility each to 9286.98 ng/mL (enhanced by 246.27 times) and light stability (enhanced by 1.51 times). The photogenerated electron transmission from Cur to CDs in addition resulted in >1.23 and 1.60 times generation of •O2 − and •OH, compared to that of bare Cur. Accordingly, 5.73 × 103 CFU L. monocytogenes, and 5.43 × 103 CFU S. aureus were killed by 0.06 mg/mL Cur-CDs within 20 mins of blue light, showing the promising potential in the development and application of safe and environmentally friendly non-thermal sterilization technology based on Cur-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curcumin nanocapsules
Hydrogen bond
electron transmission
ROS generation
Photodynamic sterilization

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Startup Foundation for Postdoctoral by Dongguan University of Technology for Xiaoqin Pan (NO. 221110165), the Regional Brand Innovation & Development Institute of Dongguan Premade Cuisine (NO. 2339139), Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization (Dalian Minzu University), Ministry of Education (KF2024007), and the Dongguan Social Development Technology Key Project (20231800938702).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process diagram of Cur-CDs nanocapsules.

(a) Digital picture of different sample in 9.09% ethanol solution (A group is aqueous solution for comparison, while B is 0.5 mg/mL CDs aqueous solution; digit 0,1,2,3 represent the injection of 1 mL of 0, 0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mg/mL ethanol solution to 10 mL reaction solution); (b) Cur aqueous solubility of different samples; (c) Cur load rate of different samples.

(a) FTIR spectra of Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2, and Cur-CDs-3; (b) FTIR spectra of CDs, Cur, physical mixture of CDs with Cur, and Cur-CDs; (c) 1HNMR spectrum of Cur; (d) 1HNMR spectrum of Cur-CDs; (e) schematic diagram of hydrogen bonds formation between CDs and Cur.

(a-d) TEM images of CDs, Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2, and Cur-CDs-3 (insert picture are the corresponding HRTEM images of single particle, and the size distribution of samples); (e) schematic diagram of morphological transformation of Cur-CDs nanocapsules.

(a) Retention rate of samples after 30 mins of blue light; (b-c) EPR spectra of detecting •O2 −and •OH signals; (d-f) UV–vis absorption spectrum; (g-i) UPS spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a–d) The absorption of bacteria after photodynamic treatment by variable concentration of Cur-CDs (n≧3, P < 0.05 and different letter means significant difference); (e–h) Sterilization results of different materials (CK: pristine bacteria).

(a-d) SEM images of untreated, L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa cells; (e-h) SEM images of Cur-CDs treated L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa cells in dark; (i-l) SEM images of and Cur-CDs photodynamic treated L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa cells.
Peak area proportions of different groups in O 1 s spectra of different samples.

head.ce:title: Curcumin Nanocapsules based on carbon dots for photodynamic sterilization towards Listeria monocytogenes and Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Health Technology, Dongguan University of Technology, Dongguan 523808, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Health Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Health Technology, Dongguan University of Technology, Dongguan, 523808, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dongguan Key Laboratory of Prepared Dishes Innovative Development & Quality Control, Regional Brand Innovation & Development Institute of Dongguan Prepared Dishes, Dongguan 523808, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dongguan Key Laboratory of Prepared Dishes Innovative Development & Quality Control
Regional Brand Innovation & Development Institute of Dongguan Prepared Dishes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dongguan Key Laboratory of Prepared Dishes Innovative Development & Quality Control, Regional Brand Innovation & Development Institute of Dongguan Prepared Dishes, Dongguan, 523808, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Life and Health Technology, Dongguan University of Technology, Dongguan 523808, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Health Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin (Cur) as a natural food additive and photosensitizer has been widely applied on photodynamic sterilization and preservation for food, but the poor aqueous solubility and light stability restrict its extensive application. In this study, we report a Cur nanocapsules (Cur-CDs) made by carbon dots (CDs). Attributing to the hydrogen bonds formed between Cur and CDs, Cur-CDs exhibits excellent Cur aqueous solubility each to 9286.98 ng/mL (enhanced by 246.27 times) and light stability (enhanced by 1.51 times). The photogenerated electron transmission from Cur to CDs in addition resulted in >1.23 and 1.60 times generation of •O2 − and •OH, compared to that of bare Cur. Accordingly, 5.73 × 103 CFU L. monocytogenes, and 5.43 × 103 CFU S. aureus were killed by 0.06 mg/mL Cur-CDs within 20 mins of blue light, showing the promising potential in the development and application of safe and environmentally friendly non-thermal sterilization technology based on Cur-CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food borne pathogens outbreak is a major public health problem, among which Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) causing food borne disease are largely prevalent(Ahmad et al., 2021; Buchanan et al., 2017). Especially, psychrotrophic L. monocytogenes is of particular concern for ready-to-eat (RTE) foods(Pennone et al., 2021). The high prevalence of RTE foods such as fresh fruit and vegetable salad is prone to the risk of contamination with L. monocytogenes(Buchanan et al., 2017). High thermal technologies for sterilization are not suitable for these RTE salad foods, while chemical sterilization technologies are not only easy to develop drug residues and environmental pollution, but also have the adverse effect on human health. For this reason, environmentally friendly non-thermal processing technology is extremely desire.

Currently, photodynamic antibacterial therapy (PDAT) as an intriguing and the most effective antibacterial technology has been widely used on food processing(Do Prado-Silva et al., 2022; Hemeg, 2017; Piksa et al., 2023). Under the light irradiation, photosensitizer generates O2 −, H2O2, and ·OH via electron transfer process (Type I), or produce 1O2 through energy transfer process (Type II)(Baptista et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2021), simultaneously causing great damages to multiple targets of cells, which is unlikely to develop drug resistance(Halliwell & Gutteridge, 2015; Richards et al., 2015; Şen Karaman et al., 2020). Based upon this, PDAT is considered as an environmentally friendly non-thermal antibacterial technology with great market application potential for fresh food products. Impressively, the sterilization effect of this technology largely depends on the properties of the photosensitizer, so the development of PDAT is focus on the exploration of efficient photosensitizers(Zhu et al., 2021).

Among numerous photosensitizers that used on food, curcumin (Cur) as a natural polyphenolic substance is most popular and has been widely used on shelf life extension of fresh-cut fruits and vegetables, due to its selective response to Uv-visible light (blue light) exposure and broad-spectrum antibacterial properties (Lan et al., 2023; Rodoni & Lemoine, 2023; Zheng et al., 2020). Unfortunately, the low aqueous solubility and bio-availability, and the poor light stability still restrict the extensive use of Cur during photodynamic sterilization. To solve these problems, many advanced technologies have been applied(Gayathri et al., 2023). Metal organic framework loading(Huang et al., 2021), silica nanoparticles coating(Saputra et al., 2023), and nanoliposome encapsulation(Liu et al., 2023) all can improve the aqueous solubility and bio availability to some extent. Nevertheless, the sensitivity of light response in those Cur nano emulsion has been adverse affected(Silva et al., 2022), which is not conductive to its photodynamic antibacterial property.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) as a new type nanomaterial with a tiny size (< 10 nm), have been developed and designed as drug loading platforms due to its good biocompatibility and surface modifiability(Pourmadadi et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023). Impressively, we noted that Cur was used to be modified on CDs to provide synergistic photodynamic and photothermal effects, in which Cur acted as functional groups on the CDs surface (Yan et al., 2021), demonstrating the favorable interaction between CDs and Cur. However, the aqueous solubility and light stability of Cur in this composite are unclear. In recent research, Pourmadadi's group introduced CDs with chitosan and gelatin as nanocarriers to a Cur delivery system for enhancing Cur half-life and cytotoxicity to brain cancer cells(Ostovar et al., 2023). Unfortunately, the specific function of CDs in this delivery system is not yet known.

Inspired by that CDs is widely used for enhancing photogenerated charge separation through hydrogen bonds during photocatalytic experiment(Liang et al., 2022), in this study, CDs with rich hydrophilic functional groups were prepared to synthesis Cur-CDs nanocapsules. We speculate that CDs not only can enhance the aqueous solubility and light stability of Cur by forming hydrogen bonds, but also can be a nice electron acceptor to repress the charge recombination of Cur. In addition, the antibacterial effect on L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, Escherichia coli (E. coli), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) by as prepared Cur-CDs nanocapsules were also evaluated. This work shows the promising potential in the development and application of safe and environmentally friendly non-thermal PDAT for RTE foods based on Cur-CDs nanocapsules.

Citric acid (CA) monohydrate was analytical reagent and purchased from Damao chemical regent factory. Chitosan was nalytical reagent with low viscosity and purchase from Rhawn. Anhydrous ethanol and Cur nalytical reagent were purchased from Aladdin. The rest of regents used in this study were all nalytical reagent grade and purchased from Macklin company.

The Cur-CDs nanocapsules were prepared according to Fig. 1
.

Firstly, the CDs was prepared by high-temperature hydrothermal method. Specifically, a total of 50 mL aqueous solution that consists of 2 g CA and 2 g chitosan was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-linedstainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After reaction cooling to room temperature, the obtained liquid was filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtrate was subsequently purify using 1000 kDa dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the dialysate was dried using a freeze dryer and redispersed in pure water.

Then, ethanol injection method was used for the encapsulation of Cur. Specifically, 1 mL of 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 mg/mL Cur ethanol solution was respectively injected into 10 mL 0.5 mg/mL CDs aqueous dispersion and stir in dark environment for 24 h.

Finally, the samples were purified by freeze centrifugation. Specifically, the ethanol in precursor was removed by a freeze concentration centrifuge at 1000 rpm. Subsequently, an equal amount of pure water was added into the centrifuge tube and centrifuge at 1000 rpm for 20 min again. The obtained supernatant was dried by a freeze dryer for later experiments.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) pictures were taken using FEI Talos F200s. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was measured on Nicolet IS50. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra was recorded by Bruker (EMXplus-6/1, Karlsruhe, Germany), in which 5.5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide was used as the trapping reagent and xenon lamp with 420 nm filter was used as light source for 10 min. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were taken on COXEM EM30. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum was measured on Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi. UV visible absorption spectrum was obtained by Thermo Evolution 220. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were conducted on Thermo Fisher using a He (hv = 21.2 eV) source. Zeta potential was conduct on Malvern Nano ZSE. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra was obtained on Bruker AVANCE NEO 400.

Different content of Cur was respectively dissolved in a 50% ethanol aqueous solution and recorded the absorption value at 434 nm to make a standard curve. The absorption at 434 nm of different sample in 50% ethanol aqueous (0.05 mg/mL) was recorded to calculate the Cur load rate.

Different content of Cur was respectively dissolved in a 95% ethanol aqueous solution and recorded the absorption value at 434 nm to make a standard curve. The samples were dispersed in 1 mL pure water and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, 0.1 mL of the supernatant was dissolved in 2 mL anhydrous ethanol and recorded the absorption at 434 nm to calculate the Cur aqueous solubility.

The sample of 0.05 mg/mL was illuminated under 468 nm LED for 30 min with the intensity of Pmax = 1 mW/cm2. After illumination, equal ethanol was added into sample liquid. The absorptions at 434 nm of sample before and after illumination were recorded to calculate the retention rate by follow formula:
(1)
Retention rate
%
=
A t
A o
× 100
where At represents adsorption after illumination while A0 represents the absorption before illumination. According to the Cur load rates of Cur-CDs samples, 2.74, 5.18, and 8.97 μg/mL Cur were used as positive comparisons. Meanwhile,0.05 mg/mL pure CDs was also used as comparison.

All the utensils required in this research were sterilized by steam under high pressure. S. aureus, E. coli, L. monocytogenes, and P. aeruginosa were respectively activated by culturing them in Luria−Bertani (LB) solution under 37 °C over night.

On one hand, the bacterial LB solution was diluted 10× with physiological saline. Subsequently, 20 μL bacterial dispersion was added into 0.98 mL physiological saline with variable contents of samples and placed under 468 nm LED light with the intensity of 1 mW/cm2 for 20 mins. After that, 20 μL bacterial dispersion was added into 2 mL LB solution and recorded the absorption at 600 nm.

On the other hand, the bacterial LB solution was diluted 1000× with physiological saline, except that L. monocytogenes was diluted 100×. Next, 20 μL bacterial dispersion was added into 0.98 mL physiological saline with variable contents of samples and placed under 468 nm LED light with the intensity of 1 mW/cm2 for 20 mins. Finally, 0.1 mL bacteria solution was used for plate counting method to evaluate the antibacterial effect.

The statistical data in this paper are analyzed by the SPSS 20.0 software. The significance of the difference between treatments was analyzed by one-way ANOVA, and the Duncan comparison test was used (P < 0.05). Origin 8.0 software was used to fit and plot the data.

Fig. S1a is the AFM image of as prepared CDs, in which the CDs has a very tiny vertical distance of 1.348 nm. Meanwhile, the dependent-excitation effect of CDs in the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum (Fig. S1b) is attributed to the surface state of O and N containing functional groups on CDs (van Dam et al., 2017), preliminary suggesting the successful synthesis of CDs as our expected. The specific hydrophilic function groups on CDs will be characterized in the subsequent FTIR and XPS spectra.

Fig. 2a is the picture of different sample after Cur injection and stirring for 24 h. Apparently, the aqueous solubility of Cur is significantly improved by CDs. Specifically, the solubility of Cur, Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 in ultrapure water (pH = 5.51) are respectively 37.71, 3137.25, 2379.68 and 9286.98 ng/mL (Fig. 2b), among which the Cur-CDs-3 shows highest Cur aqueous solubility (enhanced by 246.27 times), similar with a previous study using Lepidium sativum protein hydrolysate at pH 3.0(Kadam et al., 2019). Here the increasing aqueous solubility of Cur is likely due to the hydrogen bond formed between CDs and Cur and was later demonstrated by the followed FTIR and XPS results. Determined by spectrophotometry, the Cur load rate of Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 are 5.47%, 10.35% and 17.94% respectively (Fig. 2c). The Cur-CDs aqueous solution is in brown color, while that of Cur is yellow, possibly due to the conjugation effect of Cur induced by CDs(Boulmokh et al., 2023). The pH of Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 in ultrapure water is 4.70, 4.78 and 4.85, demonstrating that pH is unlikely as the reason for the increasing Cur aqueous solubility. Because Cur displays yellow under acidic conditions(Kadam et al., 2019).

XPS was further used to study the electron transition between Cur and CDs. As can be seen in Fig. S2, CDs are composed of C, O, and N element, while Cur only consists of C and O element. With the increasing load rate of Cur, the content of N element that originated from CDs gradually decreased. By this uniqueness, the Cur load rate of Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 were calculated to be 3.55, 10.06 and 22.19%, among which the results of Cur-CDs-2 is similar to the result that using spectrophotometry. Impressively, attributed to the protonation behavior of CO group on Cur-CDs, the peak of C=O/O-H in the high resolution XPS spectra of O1s spectra of Cur-CDs-1, Cur-CDs-2, and Cur-CDs-3 gradually positively shifts, accompanying with the increasing peak areas(Table 1
, Fig. S2)(Li et al., 2020; Pan, Fu, et al., 2023), which is agreement with the FTIR results. The C=O/O-H peak area percentage of Cur-CDs-3 are largest(55.95%), which might be the reason for the highest Cur aqueous solubility. Meanwhile, the amide N peaks of Cur-CDs-1 (399.62 eV), Cur-CDs-2 (399.56 eV), and Cur-CDs-3 (399.54 eV) are all more negative than that of CDs (399.77 eV), indicating an electron transition from Cur to CDs(Li, Kou, et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2020). Because the amide N protonation of CDs, and the CO protonation of Cur, hydrogen bond is formed between Cur and CDs (as illustrated in Fig. 3e), which is crucial for the improving aqueous solubility, light stability, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) photogenerated ability (proved by the follow EPR results). Moreover, when Cur is integrated with CDs, Cur appears as enol resonant structure, accounting for the brown color of Cur-CDs in water.

To study the encapsulation morphology of Cur-CDs, TEM and HRTEM images were taken. As can be seen in Fig. 4
a, pure CDs with good monodispersity present an average size of 1.52 nm and the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm that corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite. It is well known that CDs is of easy surface modification due to its rich surface functional groups(Xia et al., 2019). Utilizing this feature, Cur is linked and embedded in CDs through hydrogen bonding as depicted in Fig. 3. Since the Cur load rate of Cur-CDs only takes 5.47%, its morphology mainly remained the same as monodisperse CDs, and Cur displays as modified functional groups on the CDs surface ( Fig. 4
b). In contrast, Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 are capsule shapes in the TEM images ( Fig. 4
c-
4
d), among which the average size of the Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 are 15.82 and 24.15 nm on the long axis, 8.32 and 12.35 nm on the short axis. The lattice spacing of 0.22 and 0.34 nm found in Cur-CDs-2 and Cur-CDs-3 demonstrated the presence of CDs (Fig. S3), in which 0.34 nm is assigned to the (002) crystal plane of graphite. Based on the above results, the schematic diagram of morphological transformation of Cur-CDs nanocapsules is shown in Fig. 4
e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light stability and photogeneration of ROS
ce:section.ce:para: Improving the light stability of Cur is another aim of this study, because the photodegradation behavior of Cur cause restriction for its photodynamic antibacterial effects(Lan et al., 2023). As depicted in Fig. 5a, all Cur-CDs samples of 0.05 mg/mL concentration exhibit significant enhanced light stability than pure Cur under 460 nm light illumination, among which the light stability of Cur-CDs-2 is the best (enhanced by 1.39 times) and Cur-CDs-1 shows the maximum improvement (by 1.51 time). Here the improving light stability is attributed to the photoprotection behavior by the CDs that linking on Cur. In addition, the thermal stability of Cur-CDs-1 and Cur-CDs-2 are also enhanced (Fig. S4). During long-term (14 days) stability experiment under environmental light and 37 °C, Cur-CDs exhibits significantly enhanced stability than pure Cur (Fig. S5).

Considering that Cur-CDs-2 exhibits optimized light, it was selected as the final antibacterial sample. Generally, the ability of photogenerating ROS by Cur is the key process during photodynamic antibiosis. After 10 mins of xenon light illumination that cut off by a 420 nm filter, Cur-CDs exhibits significant augmented •OH EPR signals and •O2 − EPR signals of Cur-CDs than CDs and Cur (Fig. 5b-5c). Specifically, Cur-CDs produces 1.198 μM/min−1 of •O2 − and 0.397 μM/min−1 of •OH while CDs and Cur respectively produces 0.956, 0.977 μM/min−1 of •O2 − and 0.281, 0.248 μM/min−1 of •OH (Table S1). As comparison with Cur, the performance of photogenerating •O2 − and •OH of Cur-CDs are enhanced by 1.23 and 1.60 times, respectively.

To understand the mechanism of enhanced photogenerated ROS ability of Cur-CDs, Uv-vis spectrophotometer, XPS and UPS were performed. Based upon the Uv-vis absorbance spectra in Fig. 5d-5f, the band gap energy of CDs, Cur and Cur-CDs were respectively calculated to be 3.24, 2.53 and 2.51 eV, via the empirical formula of Eg = 1240/λ (Pan, Li, et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2018). Apparently, the introduction of CDs does not significantly change the band gap energy of Cur. According to the UPS results (Fig. 5g-5i), calculated by the formular 2,
(2)
Φ = hv +
E cutoff
−
E femi
where Efemi and Ecutoff were attained from the intersection point between the line fitted by the cut-off edge and the baseline(Zou et al., 2020), the work function of CDs, Cur and Cur-CDs are 4.42, 4.70, and 4.63 eV. Based up the results above, a possible mechanism for the enhanced ROS photogenerating ability of Cur-CDs could be tentatively proposed as followed. Driven by thermodynamics, the higher function of Cur than CDs prompt the photogenerated electron from Cur spontaneously transfer to CDs by the hydrogen bond, building an electron transfer pathway(Bai et al., 2022; Liang et al., 2022; Pan, Fu, et al., 2023). Thus, the work function of Cur-CDs is lower than that of CDs but higher than that of Cur. The seizing electron by CDs is favorable, due to the injected electron to the CDs suppresses the charge recombination of Cur (indicated by the decreased photoluminescence (PL) intensity than that of Cur and CDs in Fig. S6), and is gifted with the characteristics of photoinduced electron that can react with the surface-absorbed oxygen, forming a superoxide radical(Yang et al., 2011). Meanwhile, the Cur becomes positive carbon ions and react with H2O to generate •OH(Yang et al., 2011).

Thanks to the photogeneration ROS ability of Cur-CDs, it was used for photodynamic food borne sterilization. L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa were respectively mixed with 0, 0.006, 0.03, 0.06 and 0.09 mg/mL Cur-CDs PBS dispersion, and illuminated under 468 nm LED light. Indicated by the absorption at 600 nm of bacteria after different concentration of Cur-CDs photodynamic treatment in Fig. 6
a-
6
d, L
. monocytogenes is most sensitive, while S. aureus is the second, E. coli is little affected and P. aeruginosa is unaffected. From Fig. S7, the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of Cur-CDs to L. monocytogenes is 0.06 mg/mL. Based upon this concentration and the Cur load rate of Cur-CDs-2, 0.00621 mg/mL Cur and 0.05379 mg/mL CDs were used as sterilization comparisons. Without light exposure, all sample shown insignificant inhibition effect ( Fig. 6
e-
6 h). After 20 mins of blue light exposure, pure CDs presents negligible photodynamic sterilization effects, while no surviving L. monocytogenes and S. aureus were found after Cur-CDs treatment, demonstrating the necessity of light exposure. The photodynamic sterilization rates of Cur-CDs and Cur towards L. monocytogenes are all 99.99%, demonstrating that the sterilization effect is derived from the photodynamic behaviors of Cur (Fig. S8a), and 0.06 mg/mL is also the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of Cur-CDs to L. monocytogenes. Specifically, 5.73 × 103 CFU L. monocytogenes were killed. Here the stronger photodynamic anti-S. aureus and anti-E. coli effect of Cur-CDs than that of Cur as depicted in Fig. S8 are attributed to the enhancement of photogenerating ROS ability (5.43 × 103 CFU S. aureus and 2.67 × 103 CFU E. coli were killed). The increasment of S. aureus colony in Fig. 6
f after treated by CDs under light might be associated with the photo-degradation behaviors of CDs. Because CDs can be degraded into nutrients (Pan, Li, et al., 2023). Notably, L. monocytogenes and S. aureus are Gram-positive bacteria, while E. coli and P. aeruginosa are Gram-negative bacteria. The zeta potential of Cur-CDs is −21.9 mV (Fig. S9), which might be the reason for significant sterilization effects on L. monocytogenes and S. aureus.

After that, SEM was performed to observe the morphology of these three food-borne bacteria before and after treatments. From SEM images in Fig. 7
a-
7
d , normal L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, E. coli, and P. aeruginosa cells have intact cell structures with smooth membrane surface. After treated by Cur-CDs in dark for 20 mins, all the bacteria maintain a smooth surface and well disperse as untreated ( Fig. 7
e-
7 h). Significantly, after treated by Cur-CDs under blue light, the morphology of L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, and E. coli changed. Assigned to the great cell destruction effect of ROS species (Halliwell and Gutteridge, 2015; Romanazzi et al., 2017), conspicuous intercellular aggregation of L. monocytogenes, S. aureus and parts of E. coli were observed ( Fig. 7 g and 7 h), indicating the impaired cell membrane integrity, which is in accordance with a previous study(Wang et al., 2023). Regardless of whether the P. aeruginosa undergone photodynamic treatment by Cur-CDs under blue LED light, the morphology of the vast majority of them are still smooth and well dispersed, which is assigned to that the photogenerated ROS by Cur-CDs under 20 min blue light is not enough to cause distinct damage to them.

In addition, the cytotoxicity of Cur-CDs was also determined. As can be seen in Fig. S10, incubated normal human colon epithelial (NCM460) cells with different concentration of Cur-CDs that used in this study show high biocompatibility (Yang et al., 2018). The cell viability remained at 98.45% at the concentration of 0.12 mg/mL, revealing low toxicity of Cur-CDs below this concentration (Du et al., 2016). Even when the concentrations went above 0.24 mg/mL, the viability of the cells remained over 90%.

In conclusion, Cur nanocapsules was successfully prepared by using CDs and Cur. Since the hydrogen bond formed between Cur and CDs, the aqueous solubility Cur is maximally increased up to 9286.98 ng/mL (246.27 times than pure Cur), the light stability under blue light is maximally improved by 1.51 time. In addition, the higher function of Cur than CDs prompt the photoelectron from Cur spontaneously transfer to CDs by the hydrogen bond, leading to suppressed charge recombination and enhanced ROS photogenerating performance. Consequently, Cur-CDs shown excellent photodynamic antibacterial effect on L. monocytogenes and S. aureus (under blue LED light), causing ROS destruction towards bacterial cellular membrane. Nevertheless, the amount of photogenerating ROS under blue LED light is not enough to cause fatal damage to E. coli and P. aeruginosa.

Shan Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Bo Wang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Yanxue Cai: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Xuan Chen: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jihui Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Startup Foundation for Postdoctoral by Dongguan University of Technology for Xiaoqin Pan (NO. 221110165), the Regional Brand Innovation & Development Institute of Dongguan Premade Cuisine (NO. 2339139), Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Biotechnology and Bioresources Utilization (Dalian Minzu University), Ministry of Education (KF2024007), and the Dongguan Social Development Technology Key Project (20231800938702).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material: Experimental detail of MIC determination, thermal stability determination, bacteria preparation for SEM, and cytotoxicity. Supplemental results of XPS, TEM, PL spectrum, antibacterial results, and details data calculated from EPR spectra.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental detail of MIC determination, thermal stability determination, bacteria preparation for SEM, and cytotoxicity. Supplemental results of XPS, TEM, PL spectrum, antibacterial results, and details data calculated from EPR spectra. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.140295].
